hook: You already know how to graph a straight line like $y = 2x + 1$ and you know that functions assign exactly one output to each input. But what happens when a situation behaves differently depending on where you are? For example, a parking garage charges $\$3$ per hour for the first 2 hours, then $\$1$ per hour after that — the rule literally changes mid-problem. How would you write a single function that captures both rules at once?
concept: A piecewise function is a function defined by different formulas on different parts of its domain. In plain English, you follow one rule when $x$ is in one region and a different rule when $x$ is somewhere else. Formally, a piecewise function is written as $f(x) = \begin{cases} \text{formula}_1 & \text{if condition}_1 \\ \text{formula}_2 & \text{if condition}_2 \end{cases}$, where each formula applies only over its stated interval.
anatomy: A piecewise function has three key components repeated for each piece: the formula (such as $2x+1$ or $-x+4$), which is the actual algebraic rule; the condition (such as $x < 0$ or $x \geq 2$), which is the inequality describing the portion of the domain where that formula applies; and the boundary value, the specific $x$-value where one piece ends and the next begins. At a boundary, you must determine whether each piece uses an open circle (strict inequality, $<$ or $>$) or a closed circle (inclusive inequality, $\leq$ or $\geq$) on its graph.

worked_example.description_latex: Here is the full piecewise function we will evaluate and graph. Notice it has two pieces separated at the boundary $x = 1$: the first formula $2x+1$ is used whenever $x$ is strictly less than 1, and the second formula $-x+4$ is used whenever $x$ is 1 or greater.

To evaluate at $x = 0$, we first ask: which condition does $x = 0$ satisfy? Since $0 < 1$, we use the first formula $2x+1$, giving $f(0) = 1$. Always check the condition before plugging in — using the wrong formula is the most common error.
At $x = 1$, we check: $1 < 1$ is false, but $1 \geq 1$ is true, so we use $-x+4$. This gives $f(1) = 3$. The boundary value $x = 1$ belongs to the second piece because of the $\geq$ symbol.

worked_example.expression_latex: \text{At } x=1: \text{ open circle on } (1,\, 2(1)+1) = (1, 3) \text{ for piece 1; closed circle on } (1, 3) \text{ for piece 2}
worked_example.description_latex: When graphing, the boundary $x = 1$ gets an open circle on the first piece because the condition is strict ($x < 1$ means $x = 1$ is excluded), and a closed circle on the second piece because $x \geq 1$ includes $x = 1$. Notice both circles happen to land at the same $y$-value of 3, so the graph is connected here — but that won't always be the case.

For $x = 3$, the condition $x \geq 1$ is satisfied, so we use $-x + 4 = 1$. Plotting a few points on each piece and connecting them with straight lines (since both pieces are linear) completes the graph — two rays meeting at the boundary.

partial_example.description_latex: We want to evaluate $g(2)$ and $g(5)$. Start by identifying the boundary of this function — it is $x = 2$. For each input, you will need to decide which piece applies before substituting.

Since $2 \leq 2$ is true, we use the first formula. $g(2) = -2(2)+5 = 1$. Notice that if the condition had been $x < 2$, we would have used the second formula instead — the inequality symbol determines which piece owns the boundary.
Now find $g(5)$. First decide which condition $x = 5$ satisfies, then substitute into the correct formula and simplify. Write out your reasoning before computing.

Describe what happens at $x = 2$ on the graph: which piece gets a closed circle and which gets an open circle at $x = 2$, and at what $y$-value does each circle sit? Explain why the two circles are at different heights, meaning the graph has a jump here.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{Given } f(x) = \begin{cases} 3x & \text{if } x < 0 \\ x + 4 & \text{if } x \geq 0 \end{cases}, \text{ find } f(-2) \text{ and } f(0).
\text{Given } h(x) = \begin{cases} x^2 & \text{if } x < 2 \\ 2x - 1 & \text{if } x \geq 2 \end{cases}, \text{ find } h(2) \text{ and } h(-3).
\text{Write a piecewise function whose graph has slope } 1 \text{ for } x \leq 0 \text{ and slope } -2 \text{ for } x > 0, \text{ passing through the origin on each piece.}
\text{Find all values of } x \text{ such that } f(x) = 4, \text{ where } f(x) = \begin{cases} 2x - 2 & \text{if } x < 3 \\ -x + 7 & \text{if } x \geq 3 \end{cases}.
practice_problems.answer_latex: x = 3 \text{ (from piece 2) and } x = 3 \text{ is the boundary; also } x = 3 \text{ from piece 1 is excluded. Solutions: } x = 3

practice_problems.prompt_latex: A phone plan charges $\$0.10$ per minute for the first 30 minutes of a call and $\$0.05$ per minute after that. Write a piecewise function $C(m)$ for the total cost in dollars of a call lasting $m$ minutes, then find the cost of a 50-minute call.

common_mistakes: Students plug an $x$-value into the wrong formula without checking which condition it satisfies first — for example, evaluating $f(1)$ using $x < 1$ when $x = 1$ actually belongs to the $x \geq 1$ piece. Always test the inequality condition before substituting.

Students put the closed circle on the wrong piece at a boundary: a closed circle belongs to whichever piece's condition uses $\leq$ or $\geq$, and an open circle belongs to the piece using strict $<$ or $>$. Mixing these up causes the graph to misrepresent which piece actually includes the boundary point.

When a problem asks for all $x$ such that $f(x) = k$, students solve the equation in only one piece and forget to check the other piece — or they find a solution algebraically but don't verify it falls within that piece's stated domain interval, accepting an answer that isn't valid.
untested_variants: Piecewise functions with three or more pieces, such as $f(x) = \begin{cases} x+1 & x < -1 \\ 2 & -1 \leq x < 2 \\ -x+4 & x \geq 2 \end{cases}$, which require checking multiple boundary points.
Graphing a piecewise function from scratch on a coordinate plane and identifying domain and range from the resulting graph.
Piecewise functions involving absolute value or quadratic pieces, such as $f(x) = \begin{cases} x^2 & x < 0 \\ \sqrt{x} & x \geq 0 \end{cases}$.
Determining whether a piecewise function is continuous at its boundary by checking whether the left-hand value and right-hand value are equal at the boundary point.
